coredata.dc:title: Realization of bright deep-red aqueous luminescence from chlorophyll-derived CDs with hydrophilic modification for imaging application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jiahui
Huang, Wenjing
Li, Lie
Zeng, Linyun
Li, Chunli
Zhan, Qiyu
Liu, Ruiyuan
Pan, Jialiang
Huang, Jinqing
Liu, Wei
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: Chlorophyll-derived carbon dots (CDs) with strong deep red emission is an ideal candidate for high-quality bio-imaging in the NIR window. However, its low aqueous dispersity with severe emission quenching has limited its biological application. Herein, chlorophyll-derived CDs from mulberry leaf (MCDs) have been successfully coated with polyethylenimine (PEI) via a simple and mild solution-mixing method at room temperature. Experimental optimization proved that the MCDs produced at a reaction temperature of 120 °C and a reaction time of 4 h had the best PL strength, while the PEI-MCDs produced at a PEI/CDs ratio of 0.4 ‰ had the best PL strength. The PEI-MCDs composites demonstrate good aqueous dispersity and exceptional fluorescence stability under various conditions like UV irradiation, temperature fluctuations, salt and various substances. A strong narrow-band deep-red emission at 670 nm with quantum yield (QY) of 45.44 % is obtained in PEI-MCDs aqueous solution. The mechanism of lipophilic-hydrophilic transformation and emission recovery in aqueous solution is confirmed to be the amphiphilicity shell of PEI on MCDs, which effectively improves the aqueous dispersity and suppresses the occurrence of aggregation-caused quenching. Selective imaging of lysosomes in various cells has been realized by utilized PEI-MCDs without targeting ligands. Furthermore, clear tissue structures were shown in the in vivo fluorescence imaging of zebrafish embryos and larvae. The results indicate PEI-MCDs own notable amphiphilicity, superior stability and excellent deep-red emission, which hold great promise for biological imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chlorophyll-derived CDs
Polyethylenimine
Surface engineering
Aqueous dispersity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo fluorescent imaging of PEI-MCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterizations of PEI-MCDs. (a) TEM image. Inset: particle size distribution of PEI-MCDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image. (c) HRTEM lattice fringe graphs. (The inset represents the FFT and inverse FFT pattern of a single PEI-CD). (d) XRD pattern and (e) XPS spectrum. (f) FT-IR spectra of PEI-MCDs. (g) zeta potential of PEI-MCDs with various PEI/CDs ratios.

(a–e) Absorption and PL/PLE spectra of representative PEI-MCDs (4 ‰) in water, ethanol, methanol, n-butanol, and n-hexanol, respectively. (f) Photographs (taken under UV light) and PL spectra of PEI-MCDs (4 ‰) in aqueous solution, ethanol, methanol, n-butanol and n-hexanol, respectively. (g)The FWHM of different PEI/CDs ratios. (h) The PL and (I) PL E2D spectra of PEI-MCDs with different PEI/CDs ratios.

(a) Photographs of PEI-MCDs with various PEI/CDs ratios dispersed in water under daylight and UV irradiation. (b) Exciation-dependent (up) and temperature-dependent (down) PL spectra of PEI-MCDs (4 ‰) aqueous solution. (c) Comparison of λ em and QY of PEI-MCDs with reported red CDs synthesized by different precursors.

(a) Microscope images of 4T1, CT26, Hela, and MDCK cells incubated with 50 μg/mL PEI-MCDs. Object lens: 40x (b) Fluorescent images of PEI-MCDs and lyso tracker was performed at excitation wavelengths of 400 nm. Object lens: 60x.

(a) Bright field and fluorescence field images of zebrafish embryos, incubated with PEI-MCDs under different concentrations. (b) Bright-field images and fluorescence images were used to observe zebrafish cultured for 7 days with different concentrations of PEI-MCDs.
Schematic illustration of lipophilic-hydrophilic transformation and emission recovery of PEI-MCDs in aqueous solution.
Luminescent parameter comparison of PEI-MCDs with reported red CDs synthesized by different precursors and in different solvents.

head.ce:title: Realization of bright deep-red aqueous luminescence from chlorophyll-derived CDs with hydrophilic modification for imaging application

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlorophyll-derived carbon dots (CDs) with strong deep red emission is an ideal candidate for high-quality bio-imaging in the NIR window. However, its low aqueous dispersity with severe emission quenching has limited its biological application. Herein, chlorophyll-derived CDs from mulberry leaf (MCDs) have been successfully coated with polyethylenimine (PEI) via a simple and mild solution-mixing method at room temperature. Experimental optimization proved that the MCDs produced at a reaction temperature of 120 °C and a reaction time of 4 h had the best PL strength, while the PEI-MCDs produced at a PEI/CDs ratio of 0.4 ‰ had the best PL strength. The PEI-MCDs composites demonstrate good aqueous dispersity and exceptional fluorescence stability under various conditions like UV irradiation, temperature fluctuations, salt and various substances. A strong narrow-band deep-red emission at 670 nm with quantum yield (QY) of 45.44 % is obtained in PEI-MCDs aqueous solution. The mechanism of lipophilic-hydrophilic transformation and emission recovery in aqueous solution is confirmed to be the amphiphilicity shell of PEI on MCDs, which effectively improves the aqueous dispersity and suppresses the occurrence of aggregation-caused quenching. Selective imaging of lysosomes in various cells has been realized by utilized PEI-MCDs without targeting ligands. Furthermore, clear tissue structures were shown in the in vivo fluorescence imaging of zebrafish embryos and larvae. The results indicate PEI-MCDs own notable amphiphilicity, superior stability and excellent deep-red emission, which hold great promise for biological imaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, carbon dots have been widely used for bioimaging, detection probes, drug delivery, and optoelectronic devices, due to their merits like excellent fluorescence, high photo-stability, fabulous bio-compatibility and facile preparation [1–3]. Deep red/NIR CDs are particularly favorable for biomedical applications, since deep red/NIR windows could avoid light scattering and autofluorescence from tissues, offering high penetration depth, high resolution and high contrast ratio [4–6]. Yet, most of the reported deep red/NIR emitting CDs are of lipophilic and their emission are only effective in organic solvents [7–10]. The corresponding QY values are usually low (<10 %) especially when they are dispersed in water, hampering their potential as bioimaging candidate. Thus, it is of great significance to realize CDs’ high-efficient deep red/NIR emission in aqueous solution using low-cost precursors with strategies like doping and surface post-treatment.

Recently, chlorophyll-derived CDs have been widely investigated and found to be a kind of bright deep-red-emissive CDs with low toxicity and good biocompatibility, [11–15]. As revealed by Ren et al., the origin of the deep-red emission from chlorophyll-derived CDs is the molecular fluorophore chlorophyll subjected to biological micro-environmental protection [16]. However, due to the lipophilic nature of the porphyrin structure, chlorophyll-derived CDs can achieve red emission only in organic solvents such as ethanol, acetone, and DMF, while their red emission is quenched in aqueous solutions. The phenomena of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) would be observed when CDs dispersed in water. Surface engineering using hydrophilic polymers and/or small molecules is an excellent approach to improving biocompatibility and dispersity. Polyethylenimine (PEI) is an amphiphilic polymer with primary, secondary and tertiary amino groups in its skeleton, which can generate strong hydrogen bonding with water while connecting lipophilic surface groups on nano particles. Eventually, the water diversity and aqueous luminescence of CDs can be effectively improved with the surface engineering by PEI [17,18].

As demonstrated in Scheme 1, a mash and ethanol extraction process is applied on mulberry leaves, through systematic optimization experiments, then the extraction solution was put into a hydrothermal container at optimal condition of 120 °C 4 h, enabling the MCDs to achieve optimal optical properties and stability. The obtained MCDs is of lipophilic and exhibits a strong deep-red emission after dispersed in ethanol. However, aggregation and precipitation occurs when we tried to transfer MCDs to water phase. The deep-red emission is vanished due to the ACQ effect. By simply adding PEI into MCDs’ ethanol solution and stirring for 24 h, a lipophilic-hydrophilic transformation and water dispersity improvement is realized in PEI-MCDs, in which a bright deep-red emission with QE = 45.4 % in aqueous solution is found. The detailed procedures for synthesis and surface engineering of PEI-MCDs are provided in Supplementary. Optimization of reaction condition could be found in Fig. S6.

A series of characterize methods were employed to reveal PEI-MCDs’ morphology, surface chemical state, elemental composition and chemical bond configurations. This laid a solid foundation for further investigating their properties and applications. The morphology of the PEI-MCDs was examined using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the PEI-MCDs shows uniform morphology with excellent dispersion and the average lateral size is ∼4.8 nm. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 1c reveals that there are three lattice distances in PEI-MCDs, which are 0.21, 0.17 and 0.14 nm assigning to the (100), (004) and (112) plane of graphite, respectively [25,26]. The broad peaks in the XRD pattern revealed the amorphous nature of the sample. Since the sample primarily consists of amorphous carbon and a small amount of graphitic microcrystals, it is not possible to perform precise crystallographic plane indexing as with crystalline materials. The peak at 2θ = 16.7° is typically associated with amorphous cellulose [27], while the broad peak at 2θ = 23° corresponds to presence of graphitic structure in CDs. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) shows three peaks presenting C, N and O elements, showing that the atomic percentage values in PEI-MCDs are C (84.13 %), N (12.29 %) and O (3.58 %). As reported by Ref.10, the element composition of MCDs is C (78.89 %), N (0.27 %), and O (20.84 %). The elemental contents of carbon and nitrogen in PEI-MCDs are obviously higher than those in MCDs, which indicates the successful PEI surface modification on MCDs. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S2) revealed four different sub-peaks C–C/CC (284.25 eV), C–O (284.67 eV), CN (285.21 eV), and CO (285.8 eV), respectively. Meanwhile, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S3) owns two peaks corresponding to pyridinic N (N–H, 398.3 eV) and graphitic N (C–N, 398.9 eV), respectively. The O1s spectrum appears with two peaks of CO (531.5 eV) and C–O (531.07 eV, Fig. S4). Compared with MCDs, the contents of single/double-bond C and pyridinic/graphitic N in PEI-MCDs are much higher, contributing to PEI attached on MCDs surface.

The surface chemical state and functional groups of MCDs before and after PEI modification was characterized by FTIR and Raman spectra. As seen in Fig. 1f, the un-modified MCDs exhibit band at 2924 cm−1, representing aromatic rings and the long alkyl chain. The presence of hydroxide/amino group is at 3359 cm−1. At the same time, the significant peak at 1610 cm−1 stands for CC bonds in aromatic ring, that at ∼1150 cm−1 assigns to the C– O–C of the ester group, and those at 1460 and 720 cm−1 represent C–C bond stretching vibration and consecutive –CH2 rocking vibrations, respectively [11]. All these distinguished signals indicate the lipophilicity of MCDs. In the FTIR spectrum of PEI, the broad absorption band at 3523 cm−1 indicates the existence of –NH2 [28], and that at 1672 cm−1 is attributed to the –CN bending vibration. It is found that characteristic peaks of PEI could be found in PEI-MCDs, such as N–H stretching vibrations (3498 cm−1) [29], C–H/N–H bending vibrations (1471 cm−1) [30] and C–N stretching vibrations (1116∼1045 cm−1) [31], which confirms the successfully PEI surface modification on MCDs and good solubility of PEI-MCDs in water. In addition, the characteristic absorption bands of the amino group (-NH2) were blueshifted from 3523 cm−1 to 3498 cm−1 (Fig. S1), while –CN was blueshifted from 1672 cm−1 to 1556 cm−1. These blue-shift bands provide an evidence of hydrogen bonding between hydroxide group on MCDs surface and amino group of the PEI molecule under the present conditions [32]. Thus, water solubility, biocompatibility and luminescence efficiency of MCDs in aqueous solution would be improved by PEI passivation.

As a piece of auxiliary evidence, the successful PEI surface engineering on MCDs could be further verified by zeta potential variation (Fig. 1g). A negative value is found in un-modified MCDs, then the zeta potential becomes positive and increases with PEI/CDs ratio with its maximum of +17 eV at ratio of 5 ‰. Further increase of PEI/CDs ratio resulted in a decrease in potential value (Fig. S5). The amino groups present in PEI molecules can dissociate in water into NH4 + and OH− ions [33]. These ions can form hydrogen bonds and ionic bonds with water molecules, facilitating effective interaction between PEI molecules and water [34]. Additionally, the amino groups in the structure of PEI molecules can attract hydrogen bond donors or acceptors in water molecules, enhancing their solubility and dispersibility in water, thereby exhibiting strong hydrophilicity [35]. Therefore, the hydrophilicity of PEI increases with an increasing number of amino groups in its molecules, as more amino groups imply the molecule can form more hydrogen bonds or ionic bonds with water molecules, thereby increasing its interaction ability with water [36]. When PEI/CDs ratio is above the optimal content, it may lead to excessive aggregation or polymerization, which could alter the surface structure of PEI-MCDs. After sufficient coverage of the CDs surface by PEI, further increases in PEI may lead to excessive aggregation or polymerization that could alter the surface structure of PEI-MCDs, which is presented by charge neutralization [37].

Solvent is a key factor in determining the luminescence properties of CDs. In this study, five common solvents were selected: water, ethanol, methanol, n-butanol and n-hexanol. These solvents possess distinct physical and chemical properties, providing diverse environmental conditions for PEI-MCDs. Equal concentrations of PEI-MCDs were dispersed in the same volume of these solvents, and the corresponding absorption/PL/PLE spectra were measured (Fig. 2 a–e). The absorption spectrum shows a broad absorption with maximum peak at 400 nm. This could be attributed to the π→π∗ transition in aromatic ring of chlorophyll. Emission spectra typically consisted of two peaks, i.e. a dominant one at 676 nm and an acromion at 725 nm. It is well-known that the PL that arises from the CDs molecular fluorophore can be affected or diminished by the significant interaction between the solvent molecules and the CDs surface. In this scenario, the maximum absorption wavelength, emission spectral shape and the relative intensities of PEI-MCDs are found to be independent to the polarity of the solvents. These indicate that the interaction between CDs surface and solvent molecules can be passivated by PEI surface modification, which stabilizes molecular fluorophore chlorophyll in MCDs. Fig. 2f shows the photos of PEI-MCDs dissolved in various solvents under UV irradiation, in which bright deep-red emission can be clearly seen in all solutions.

To further investigate the PEI effect on luminescence of PEI-MCDs, a series of PEI-MCDs solutions with linearly varying PEI/CDs ratios were prepared by mixing CDs and PEI at different concentrations, then solving in water with same absorbance of 0.1. The ratio of the PL and PLE spectra as a function of PEI/CDs ratio is shown in Fig. 2g. The PL intensity rises gradually with increasing PEI/CDs ratio, while the dominant PL peak is almost constant with a slight spectra broadening. A 13-fold boost in integrated intensity of PEI-MCDs (4 ‰) is achieved compared to PEI-MCDs (1 ‰), while both keep almost the same spectral shape and FWHM value. Further increase of PEI concentration results in PL/PLE decline, which is consistent with the change tendency of zeta potential. Fig. 2h and i displays the contour plots of emission and excitation for PEI-MCDs samples with linearly varying PEI/CDs ratios, respectively. It is obvious that the excitation and emission profiles remain constant, and no spectra broadening is presented at elevated PEI ratio. PEI-MCDs show the maximum PLE intensity at 5 ‰ and PL intensity at 4 ‰, while both keep almost the same FWHM variation during the whole range of PEI/CDs ratio from 1 to 8 ‰. Interestingly, the PEI surface modification leads to an improvement against water quenching, indicating that the PEI as protective shell helps to stabilize the molecular fluorophore to resist solvent stress.

Fig. 3 a shows the photos of PEI-MCDs with various PEI/CDs ratios dispersed in water under daylight and UV irradiation. It is clearly seen that the unmodified CDs would aggregate and precipitate after transferred to water phase, and the luminescence is totally quenched. With PEI modification, all PEI-MCDs with various PEI/CDs ratios have uniform dispersion in water and a series of homogeneous solutions are formed. Meanwhile, the recovery of deep-red emission could be observed at UV excitation, in which the brightest one is PEI-MCDs (4 ‰). The 2D PL spectra (Fig. 3b) show that the spectral shape of PEI-MCDs does not change with the shift of excitation wavelength from 360–550 nm. This result shows that PEI-MCDs possess highly stable emission with independent of the excitation wavelength. This "wavelength stability" of luminescence indicates that molecular state of PEI-MCDs are uniform and beneficial to maintaining stable emission characteristics.

To comprehensively assess stability characteristics and application potential of PEI-MCDs, a series of experiments about the emission variation against heat, ions, salts and UV-irradiation were conducted to test PEI-MCDs’ performance. Fig. 3b also shows the 2D temperature PL of PEI-MCDs (4 ‰) aqueous solution measured in the range of 25∼80 °C. PL intensity maintains 75 % of the initial value at 80 °C, while the FWHM keeps almost the same during the heating process. In practical applications, fluorescent imaging agents may encounter various interfering substances such as metal ions and anions. The anti-interference ability of PEI-MCDs was investigated, in which a series of metal ions and anions were used. The results (Fig. S7) showed various ions had minimal impact on the fluorescence intensity of PEI-MCDs, indicating PEI-MCDs’ good anti-interference ability. As shown in Fig. S8, there is no significant fluctuation in PEI-MCDs PL intensity under different NaCl concentrations (0–10 mM), which certificates PEI-MCDs application potential in physiological salt environments. To test anti-photobleaching performance, PEI-MCDs solution was placed under a 365 nm UV lamp (12 W) and continuously irradiated for 90 min. It is found that the PL intensity remains steady after 90 min UV irradiation (Fig. S9). These results indicate that PEI-MCDs can maintain their excellent optical properties under various environmental factors. Moreover, the PEI-MCDs showed excellent long-term storage stability, with no significant change in PL intensity after one week of storage at 4 °C. The stability of the PL intensity was further verified by calibrating the absorbance of the samples to 0.18, as supported by the data analysis in Fig. S10.

To understand the fluorescence kinetics of PEI-MCDs, time-resolved PL decay was measured and shown in Fig. S11. The fitted biexponential decay curves has two components, the fast one (τ1) is closely related to non-radiative recombination of photoexcited carriers, and the slow one (τ2) is due to the radiative recombination of excitons. The average decay lifetime of the PEI-MCDs varies from 4.45 to 5.53 ns, which is related the PEI ratio. Notably, when the PEI/CDs ratio was 5 ‰, the lifetime reached its maximum value, and the quantum efficiency was also optimized. In these cases the longer average times of recombination indicate the improved role of radiative pathways as supported by enhanced PL QY. Indeed, longer excited-state lifetimes govern increased possibility of radiative recombinations. Furthermore, the PL decay lifetimes align with the PL intensity trends. The absolute QY of PEI-MCDs was obtained using integrating sphere (Fig. S12), in which PEI-MCDs water solution with absorbance of ∼0.1 is used under 400 nm excitation. The QY was measured as 45.4 %, which is higher than that of most reported red CDs (3–30 %). Fig. 3c and Table 1 summarize the precursors, solvent, excitation/emission peak wavelength and QY values of PEI-MCDs and representative red CDs, respectively. It shows that PEI-MCDs owns the highest QY and the longest emission wavelength among red CDs dissolved in water. Surprisingly, PEI-MCDs exhibits a comparative performance with reported red CDs in organic solvents, making it of great potential as fluorescent bio-imaging agent.

Knowing luminescence mechanisms is of importance in guiding synthesis and promoting applications of CDs. Presently, there are three categories of CDs’ luminescence mechanisms: surface state, carbon core state, and molecular state. Among them, molecular state refers to the molecular fluorophores that are connected with graphite core of CDs and generally endows CDs with a strong luminescence and a high QE. Scheme 2 illustrates the possible mechanism about lipophilic-hydrophilic transformation and emission recovery of PEI-MCDs in aqueous solution. Chlorophyll confined by lipophilic surface group that attached to graphite core is the main luminescent center of MCDs. Due to lipophilicity of MCDs, it exhibits a deep-red emission with narrow FWHM and high QY only in organic solvents with lipophilic alkyl group or aromatic rings. After transferred to water, this lipophilic MCDs would automatically accumulate and result om serious luminescence quenching by ACQ effect. When the MCDs are surface-engineered with PEI, the surface state is transformed from lipophilic to hydrophilic, i.e. the lipophilic end of PEI molecule is connected to the lipophilic surface groups of MCDs through inter-molecular interactions. Meanwhile, the hydrophilic end of PEI molecule is retained on the outside and forming hydrogen bonds with water. An amphiphilic protective shell of PEI is formed and coated on the MCDs, which improves the water dispersivity of PEI-MCDs, prevents the ACQ occurrence and regains its strong deep-red emission in aqueous solution. Besides, it is known that molecular fluorophores are normally less stable and therefore vulnerable to exterior factors. After subjected to PEI shell protection, the structure of chlorophyll remains intact under heat, ions, salts and UV-irradiation. It still acts as an emitting center and exhibits deep-red emission.

To further validate luminescence mechanisms, according to Fig. S13, It has to be noted that chlorophyll extracted from mulberry leaves is not soluble in water. So the study is carried out by dissolving it into other organic solvents with varying polarity and viscosity. The experimental results indicate that the extracted chlorophyll has approximately equal QY and fluorescence lifetimes in methanol, acetone, and dichloromethane within experimental error, whereas these data are reduced in ethylene glycol due to aggregation-caused quenching led by increasing solvent polarity. These findings support the need of the PEI coating to increase CDs'dispersion in polarity solvent and suppress ACQ effect. The amphiphilic structure of PEI not only improves the dispersibility of MCDs in water but also restores their strong emission properties in aqueous solution.

Lysosomes are important structures for maintaining normal cell function and health [19], which play a key role in several aspects of intracellular substance metabolism, waste disposal, immune defense, and apoptosis [20]. Numerous methods, like Raman spectroscopy, atomic absorption and electrochemical method, have been developed to directly visualize and investigate LDs-lysosomes interplay. However, the above methods require expensive instruments and experienced operators, which are not suitable for on-site real-time analyses. Fluorescent probes have been raised as powerful tools to decipher the dynamics and functions of organelles in live cells, because of merits of noninvasive detection, fast response and high sensitivity [21–24]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop novel fluorescent probes to visualize lysosomes and realize real-time tracking on their distribution and interplay.

Benefiting from high QY, narrow FWHM deep-red emission and good hydrophilicity, PEI-MCDs are considered as ideal probe for live cell imaging and lysosome targeting. To evaluate the biocompatibility of PEI-MCDs, MTT assays were performed by co-incubating PEI-MCDs at different concentrations (0–100 μg/ml) with 4T1, CT26, Hela and MDCK for a treatment duration of 24 h. More than 50 % cell survival at 50 μg/ml of PEI-MCDs (Fig. S14) confirms PEI-MCDs possess low cytotoxicity and suitable applications for cell imaging. To evaluate the feasibility of the lysosome-targeting PEI-MCDs, the confocal microscopy was applied to observe the distribution of PEI-MCDs in 4T1, CT26, HeLa, and MDCK cells. Fig. 4 a shows the CLSM images of cells incubated with 50 μg/mL PEI-MCDs for 24 h. In addition, it can be observed that the fluorescence predominantly accumulates around the nucleus, suggesting localization within organelles such as mitochondria and lysosomes [48]. To investigate the subcellular distribution of PEI-MCDs, co-localization experiments were performed using a commercially available lysosomal dye (Lyso-Tracker Green) for further characterization. The lysosomal localization dye emits green fluorescence upon excitation with a 488 nm laser. As shown in Fig. 4b, a great signal overlap is observed between the red fluorescence from PEI-MCDs and the green one from Lyso-Tracker Green, verifying the lysosome targeting ability of PEI-MCDs [49]. Due to its ability to form complexes with DNA or RNA, PEI complexes are taken up by cells through endocytosis and subsequently transported through various cellular compartments to reach lysosomes. Vesicles formed during endocytosis fuse with lysosomes over time, releasing their cargo into the lysosomal environment [17,50,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescent imaging of PEI-MCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the in vivo imaging capability of PEI-MCDs in zebrafish embryos and larvae is studied to widen their applications. After incubating the embryos with different concentrations of PEI-MCDs, significant changes in fluorescence signals were observed. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the brightness of the images increased with the concentration of PEI-MCDs. This indicates that PEI-MCDs can penetrate the chorion of zebrafish embryos and distribute effectively within them, particularly in the yolk sac area. The fluorescence intensity of the yolk sac showed a clear concentration-dependent enhancement. These results confirmed the excellent imaging capabilities of PEI-MCDs in zebrafish embryos and larvae.

Zebrafish larvae were also used as a biological model to validate the use of PEI-MCDs as fluorescent imaging probes for in vivo imaging and to examine the tissue distribution of CDs. Images were recorded of the larvae immersed in the hybrid PEI-MCDs solution at different concentrations for 15 min (Fig. 5b). Whole-body images confirmed that PEI-MCDs successfully entered the larvae, with bright red emission observed in a concentration-dependent manner. The concentration-dependent emission and varying tissue affinity demonstrated that PEI-MCDs could enter the larvae through skin absorption and ingestion. The fluorescent images showed increased brightness with higher PEI-MCDs concentrations.

In this work, PEI-modified chlorophyll-derived CDs composite has been successfully fabricated via a simple, mild but effective solution-mixing method at room temperature. PEI-MCDs composites have remarkable hydrophilicity enhancement after PEI-coating and exhibits a high-efficient deep-red emission at 670 nm with QY = ∼50 % in aqueous solution. We found that chlorophyll-dominated fluorescence could be passivated after an amphiphilic protective shell of PEI is formed and coated on MCDs. PEI-MCDs shows favorable traits such as notable hydrophilicity, high-QY deep-red emission, superior stability, commendable biocompatibility and specific lysosome targeting ability. After incubating PEI-CDs with 4T1, Hela, CT26, and MDCK cells, it is verified that PEI-MCDs exhibit excellent cellular-imaging and lysosome targeting capabilities. Additionally, the successful application in zebrafish embryos and larvae further confirmed the great potential of PEI-MCDs in live imaging. These studies not only demonstrated the outstanding performance of aqueous-phase high QY PEI-MCDs, but also laid the foundation for their broad application in the biomedical field.

Jiahui Zhang: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Wenjing Huang: Conceptualization. Lie Li: Investigation. Linyun Zeng: Resources, Investigation. Chunli Li: Investigation. Qiyu Zhan: Investigation. Ruiyuan Liu: Resources. Jialiang Pan: Resources. Jinqing Huang: Resources, Investigation. Wei Liu: Resources. Xinguo Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no competing interests with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled, “Realization of bright deep-red aqueous luminescence from chlorophyll-derived CDs with hydrophilic modification for imaging application”.
We declare that we have no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research is funded by the Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA080005), Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260). The corresponding author would like to express his thanks to Prof. Ruiyuan Liu and Prof. Wei Liu for providing research resource, and to two lovely and naughty sons (Zhiping Zhang & Zhibang Zhang) who always give inspiration and responsibility to their father.
